Learning Objectives
By the end of today, you will be able to:

Describe the transformer architecture at a conceptual level and why it enabled the current AI revolution

Use AI to assist with professional writing tasks relevant to public health practice

title: Day 1: Foundations of AI
text:
Lecture: AI Concepts and History

What Is Artificial Intelligence?
AI is a broad field encompassing any system that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence. We will walk through the key layers:

Artificial Intelligence (AI): The broadest category—machines that can sense, reason, act, and adapt
Machine Learning (ML): Systems that learn patterns from data rather than following explicit rules

Supervised learning: Learning from labeled examples (e.g., classifying chest X-rays)
Unsupervised learning: Finding patterns without labels (e.g., clustering neighborhoods by health outcomes)
Reinforcement learning: Learning through trial and reward (e.g., optimizing treatment protocols)

Deep Learning: ML using neural networks with many layers, enabling learning from raw data (images, text, audio)
Foundation Models / Large Language Models (LLMs): Models trained on massive datasets that can be adapted to many tasks



The Transformer Revolution
In 2017, the paper “Attention Is All You Need” introduced the transformer architecture, which enabled:

Processing entire sequences in parallel (not word by word)
The “attention mechanism”—allowing models to focus on relevant parts of the input
Scaling to billions of parameters, creating increasingly capable models

This architecture underlies ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and essentially all modern LLMs.


Why Urban Health Needs AI
Urban public health generates enormous amounts of data—surveillance records, community surveys, environmental monitoring, clinical data, policy documents—yet many departments are resource-constrained. AI can help with:

Rapid literature synthesis and evidence review
Data analysis and visualization with limited statistical staff
Drafting reports, communications, and policy briefs
Identifying patterns in large datasets
Translating materials for diverse communities
Streamlining routine administrative tasks







TipKey Concept: AI as a Tool, Not a Replacement



AI is most effective when it augments human expertise rather than replacing it. Throughout this course, we will focus on human-in-the-loop approaches where AI assists with tasks but humans make the final decisions.




A Working Vocabulary
A few terms you will hear all week: a model is the trained system itself (Claude, GPT, Gemini) — a pattern-completion engine, not a database; a token is a chunk of text (~¾ of a word) counted for usage and limits; a prompt is what you send, including any context; the context window is how much it can hold in mind at once; a session is one conversation (no memory across sessions unless you build it); and an agent is a model given tools and a goal that it pursues in steps (Day 3).


Three Ways to Work With AI
The same model behaves differently depending on where you meet it: in a chat window (browser; ask, paste, generate; can’t see your files), inside your tools (an editor or app that sees and edits your work in place), or agentically (you give a goal and it plans and runs multi-step work). The move from “ask and copy” to “delegate and verify” is the biggest shift this year.


The Three Inverse Laws of AI
Asimov’s laws constrain robots to protect people; these “inverse laws” constrain us: non-anthropomorphism (don’t attribute judgment or intent to the model), non-deference (never treat output as authoritative without verification), and non-abdication (you remain responsible for the result — “the AI told me so” is never a defense). We return to these on Day 5.
Vocabulary, the three modes, and the inverse laws are adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).


Philadelphia AI Policy Discussion
The Commonwealth of Pennsylvania has issued executive guidance on AI use in state agencies. We will review:

Current state-level AI governance frameworks
How Philadelphia city agencies are approaching AI adoption
Implications for your daily work and data handling

Activity 1: Explore Generative AI Platforms

Activity 1: Explore Generative AI Platforms

Duration: ~40 minutes | Format: Individual exploration + group discussion

Goal: Get hands-on experience with multiple AI platforms and understand their strengths and differences.

Instructions

Open each of the following platforms (you should have created accounts before today):

ChatGPT (OpenAI)
Claude (Anthropic)
Gemini (Google)
Perplexity

Ask each platform the same prompt and compare responses:

Prompt 1 (Factual):

What are the leading causes of premature mortality in Philadelphia, and how do they differ by neighborhood?

Prompt 2 (Analytical):

I work at a city health department. Summarize the evidence on heat-related illness prevention strategies that have been effective in urban areas similar to Philadelphia.

Prompt 3 (Creative):

Draft three social media posts (for X/Twitter) promoting free lead testing for children in North Philadelphia. The tone should be warm, urgent but not alarming, and culturally appropriate.


Compare and document your findings using this table:




Feature
ChatGPT
Claude
Gemini
Perplexity




Response quality






Citations/sources






Length/detail






Tone/style






Factual accuracy






Unique strengths









Discussion Questions

Which platform gave the most accurate response? How did you verify?
Which platform cited its sources most clearly?
Did any platform refuse to answer or add caveats? Why might that be?
Which would you reach for first for different types of tasks?









TipPlatform Differences to Watch For




ChatGPT has a large ecosystem of plugins and custom GPTs; strong at creative and conversational tasks
Claude tends toward longer, more nuanced responses with explicit reasoning; strong safety guardrails
Gemini integrates with Google Workspace and has strong multimodal capabilities
Perplexity is purpose-built for research with inline citations from web sources

title: Day 1: Foundations of AI
text:
Activity 2: Prompt Engineering Practice

Activity 2: Prompt Engineering Practice

Duration: ~40 minutes | Format: Guided practice with partner discussion

Goal: Learn and practice the PTCF framework for writing effective prompts.

The PTCF Framework
Good prompts have four components:








Component
Description
Example




Persona
Who should the AI act as?
“You are a public health epidemiologist…”


Task
What specifically should it do?
“Write a 500-word executive summary…”


Context
What background information is needed?
“This is for the city council health committee…”


Format
How should the output be structured?
“Use bullet points with headers, include a recommendation section…”





Practice Prompts
Try these prompts using the PTCF framework. Start with the “basic” version, then improve it:
Scenario 1: Herd Immunity Explanation
Basic prompt: “Explain herd immunity.”
Improved prompt (try writing your own first, then compare):

Persona: You are a community health educator working in a diverse urban neighborhood in Philadelphia.
Task: Explain the concept of herd immunity to a group of parents at a community health fair.
Context: Many parents in this community have questions about childhood vaccinations. Reading levels vary widely. Some community members have expressed vaccine hesitancy based on misinformation on social media.
Format: Write a 200-word explanation at an 8th-grade reading level. Use an analogy. End with one clear call to action. Avoid medical jargon.

Scenario 2: Vaccination Social Media Campaign
Basic prompt: “Write a social media post about flu shots.”
Your improved PTCF version: Write a prompt that specifies the audience (e.g., elderly residents in South Philadelphia), the platform (e.g., Facebook), the tone, and the desired call to action.
Scenario 3: Air Quality Executive Summary
Basic prompt: “Summarize air quality data.”
Your improved PTCF version: Specify who you are (e.g., environmental health analyst), what data you are summarizing (e.g., PM2.5 readings from 2024-2025 across Philadelphia neighborhoods), who the audience is (e.g., City Council), and what format the summary should take (e.g., 1-page brief with key findings, a table, and policy recommendations).


Advanced Techniques
After practicing the basics, try these:

Chain-of-thought prompting: Add “Think through this step by step” to complex analytical questions
Few-shot prompting: Give 1–2 examples of the output you want before asking for a new one
Structured output: Ask for JSON, CSV, or specific table formats
Iterative refinement: Start with a draft prompt, review the output, and revise the prompt







NoteReproducibility Note



AI outputs are not perfectly reproducible—the same prompt can generate different responses each time. For work that requires consistency, save your prompts, document which model and version you used, and note the date. Some platforms offer a “temperature” setting that affects randomness.

title: Day 1: Foundations of AI
text:
Activity 3: AI for Professional Writing

Activity 3: AI for Professional Writing

Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Individual work + pair sharing

Goal: Practice using AI as a writing assistant for real public health work products.

Instructions
Choose one of the following tasks (or bring your own de-identified writing task):
Option A: Draft a Public Health Report Section
Ask AI to help you draft a section of a report on a topic relevant to your work. For example:

You are a public health analyst at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Draft the “Background” section (300–400 words) for a report on childhood asthma rates in Philadelphia. Include relevant national and local statistics, known risk factors in urban environments, and health equity considerations. Cite sources where possible and flag any statistics that should be verified.

Option B: Edit and Improve Existing Writing
Paste a paragraph from a real (de-identified) work document and ask the AI to:

Improve clarity and readability
Adjust the tone for a specific audience
Identify gaps in the argument
Suggest stronger transitions

Option C: Write a Policy Brief

Draft a 1-page policy brief recommending that Philadelphia expand its network of cooling centers based on heat vulnerability data. Include: an executive summary (2–3 sentences), the problem statement, key evidence, and 3 specific recommendations. The audience is City Council members who are not public health experts.



Pair Discussion
After completing your writing task, share with a partner:

What prompt did you use? How did you refine it?
What did the AI do well? What needed human editing?
Would you use this approach in your actual work? Why or why not?









WarningReminder: De-Identify Your Data



If you bring a real work document to this activity, make sure it contains no protected health information (PHI), personally identifiable information (PII), or confidential agency data. When in doubt, create a fictional scenario based on your real work.

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Lecture: Agents, Context, and Tools

Chatbots vs. Agents
The AI landscape is rapidly moving from chatbots (reactive, single-turn, limited to text) to agents (proactive, multi-step, tool-using):








Feature
Chatbot
Agent




Interaction
Single turn Q&A
Multi-step task completion


Tools
None (text only)
Can use tools (search, code, APIs)


Memory
Limited to conversation
Can maintain context across tasks


Autonomy
Responds only when asked
Can take initiative, plan, and execute


Error handling
Stops at confusion
Can retry, adapt, self-correct



Why this matters for public health: Agents can do things like “pull the latest flu surveillance data, compare it to last year, draft a summary report, and email it to the team”—tasks that involve multiple steps, tools, and decision points.


Context Engineering: The 5 Components
Context engineering is the practice of designing the information environment that an AI system operates within. It is more fundamental than prompt engineering. The five key components:

System prompt / Instructions: What the AI should do, its role, its constraints
User input: The specific request or query
Retrieved knowledge: Relevant documents, data, or information pulled in for the task (RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Tool definitions: What tools the AI can use and how to use them
Conversation history / Memory: What has happened before in the interaction







TipThink of It Like Briefing a New Employee



Context engineering is like preparing a briefing packet for a highly skilled new employee on their first day. You would not just say “do your job.” You would provide: their role description (system prompt), the specific task (user input), relevant background materials (retrieved knowledge), access to the tools and systems they need (tool definitions), and notes from previous meetings (conversation history).




Model Context Protocol (MCP)
MCP is an emerging open standard (created by Anthropic) that allows AI models to connect with external tools and data sources through a standardized interface. Think of it as a “USB port for AI”—a universal way to plug in capabilities.
Examples of MCP tools:

File system access (read/write documents)
Database queries
Web search
Calendar and email integration
Code execution
API calls to external services



Verification Is the Bottleneck
Agents generate far faster than you can check, so verification — not generation — becomes the limiting step and the real skill. Fluent output is not the same as correct output, and the higher the stakes, the higher your burden of proof. One of the most reliable uses of a model is to turn it on its own work: ask it to be an adversarial reader (“argue why this analysis is wrong”), to find the weakest claim, the missing control, or the unchecked assumption in your code, draft, or study design — then you decide.
Adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise

Activity 1: Context Engineering Exercise

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Small group design exercise

Goal: Design a context package for a specialized public health AI assistant.

Scenario
You are designing a “Neighborhood Health Assessment AI Assistant” for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. This agent should be able to:

Pull demographic and health data for any Philadelphia neighborhood
Compare indicators across neighborhoods
Generate assessment reports with visualizations
Identify health equity concerns
Recommend evidence-based interventions



Your Task
In your small group, design the five context components for this agent:
1. System Prompt / Instructions

What role should the agent assume?
What are its core capabilities?
What constraints should it have? (e.g., never provide individual-level data, always cite sources, flag uncertainty)

2. User Input Examples

Write 3 example queries a health department analyst might ask this agent

3. Retrieved Knowledge

What documents, datasets, or knowledge bases should this agent have access to?
How often should they be updated?

4. Tool Definitions

What tools should this agent be able to use? (e.g., database query, mapping, chart generation, report writing)

5. Conversation History / Memory

What should the agent remember across conversations?
What should it forget (for privacy/security)?



Share Out
Each group presents their context design in 2 minutes. Class discusses: What did groups prioritize differently? What components were hardest to define?

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough

Activity 2: Claude Code CLI Walkthrough

Duration: ~25 minutes | Format: Instructor demonstration (follow along if you have Claude Code installed)

Goal: See how a command-line AI tool can perform data analysis, write code, and generate documents directly on your computer.

What is Claude Code?
Claude Code is a command-line interface (CLI) tool from Anthropic that lets you interact with Claude directly from your terminal. Unlike the web-based chat, Claude Code can:

Read and write files on your computer
Execute code and see results
Navigate your file system
Chain multiple operations together
Work with local data that never leaves your machine (though prompts are still sent to Anthropic’s servers)



Demo Outline
The instructor will demonstrate:
1. Data Analysis and Visualization
claude "Load the Philadelphia HVI dataset from data/hvi.csv.
       Create a summary by zip code and generate a bar chart
       of the top 10 most vulnerable areas. Save the chart
       as hvi_top10.png"
2. Document Generation
claude "Read the analysis results in output/ and draft a
       2-page executive summary for city council. Include
       the key findings, a table of the most vulnerable
       neighborhoods, and three policy recommendations.
       Save as executive_summary.docx"
3. Multi-Step Workflow
claude "Download the latest flu surveillance data from CDC,
       compare Philadelphia's rates to the national average,
       create a trend visualization, and draft a weekly
       update email for the department director"


Key Takeaways

CLI tools are powerful for integrating AI into existing workflows
They work with your local files and tools
They enable automation of repetitive analysis-report-communicate cycles
They require some comfort with the command line (but AI can help with that too)

section: Activity 4: Google AI Studio Agent Building
text:
Activity 4: Google AI Studio Agent Building

Activity 4: Google AI Studio Agent Building

Duration: ~30 minutes | Format: Hands-on (using your free Google AI Studio account)

Goal: Build a simple AI agent with system instructions and structured behavior using Google AI Studio.

Instructions

Go to Google AI Studio
Click “Create New” and select a structured prompt or chat-based prompt



Option A: Disease Prediction Agent
Build an agent that helps public health analysts assess disease outbreak risk:
System Instructions:

You are an infectious disease risk assessment assistant for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Your role is to help analysts evaluate potential outbreak risks based on surveillance data and environmental factors.
When analyzing a potential outbreak: 1. Ask clarifying questions about the data (number of cases, location, time period, demographics affected) 2. Compare to historical baselines 3. Identify potential risk factors 4. Assess the likelihood of sustained transmission 5. Recommend specific response actions based on Philadelphia’s outbreak response protocols
Always cite the evidence basis for your assessments. Always flag uncertainty. Never provide definitive predictions—use probabilistic language.

Test your agent with scenarios like: > “We’ve seen 15 cases of Legionella in the Kensington area over the past two weeks. The previous baseline for this period is 2-4 cases. What should we be looking at?”


Option B: Environmental Health Risk Assessor
Build an agent focused on environmental health:
System Instructions:

You are an environmental health risk assessment assistant. You help public health analysts evaluate environmental exposures and their potential health impacts in Philadelphia neighborhoods.
For each assessment: 1. Identify the exposure pathway (air, water, soil, food) 2. Assess the exposed population (size, vulnerability factors) 3. Evaluate exposure duration and intensity 4. Review known health effects from the scientific literature 5. Recommend monitoring, mitigation, and communication strategies
Always specify the strength of evidence for health effects. Distinguish between established and emerging science.



Reflection

How does providing system instructions change the AI’s behavior?
What guardrails did you include? What would you add?
How could this be useful in your daily work?
What are the risks of relying on such an agent?

Activity 5: AI for Grant Writing

Where AI Can Help








Grant Component
How AI Can Assist

Background

Quick Practice
Try one of these prompts:

I am writing an R01 grant proposal to study the relationship between urban green space and mental health outcomes in Philadelphia. Draft a 1-page Specific Aims section with: a brief introductory paragraph establishing the problem, an overarching goal, and three specific aims. Each aim should include a brief description and a hypothesis.

Then critically evaluate: What would you keep? What needs your expertise to fix?

NIH and other funders are developing policies on AI use in grant applications. As of 2025, NIH requires disclosure of AI tool use in the preparation of grant applications. Always check the current funder requirements before submitting. AI cannot serve as a reviewer or be listed as a co-investigator.

title: Day 3: Agentic AI & Tool Building
text:
Activity 6: Local AI with Ollama

Activity 6: Local AI with Ollama

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Demonstration + optional hands-on installation

Goal: Understand why local AI models matter for sensitive data and see how to run them.

Why Local Models?
When you use ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, your prompts and data are sent to external servers. For many public health tasks, this is fine. But for work involving:

Protected Health Information (PHI)
Personally identifiable data
Pre-publication research data
Sensitive agency communications
Data covered by IRB protocols

…you may need AI that runs entirely on your own machine, with no data leaving your computer.


What is Ollama?
Ollama is a free, open-source tool that lets you run AI models locally on your laptop. It supports many models, including:

Llama 3.x / Llama 4 (Meta) — good general-purpose models
Gemma 4 (Google) — lightweight and capable; runs well on a laptop
Qwen / DeepSeek — strong open models (note: developed in China; data stays local when run via Ollama)
Mistral — fast and efficient
Phi (Microsoft) — optimized for smaller hardware



Installation (for those who can install software)
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install ollama

# Or download from https://ollama.com/download

# Start Ollama
ollama serve

# Pull and run a model
ollama run llama3
System Requirements:

At least 8 GB RAM (16 GB recommended)
5–10 GB free disk space per model
macOS, Linux, or Windows



Browser-Based Alternatives (for restricted computers)
If you cannot install software on your work computer:

Google AI Studio: Free, browser-based, runs Gemini models with reasonable privacy policies
HuggingChat: Free, open-source models, browser-based
Note: These still send data to external servers but may have stronger privacy protections than consumer products



Demo
The instructor will demonstrate:

Installing and running Ollama
Chatting with a local model
Comparing response quality to cloud-based models (trade-offs: speed, quality, privacy)
When local models are “good enough” vs. when you need cloud models







NoteFor IT-Restricted Computers



Many government and health department computers restrict software installation. If you cannot install Ollama on your work computer, the browser-based alternatives above provide a middle ground. For truly sensitive work, talk to your IT department about setting up a local AI environment on a secure server.

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
section: Lecture: Responsible AI for Public Health
text:
Lecture: Responsible AI for Public Health

FAIR Data Principles and AI
The FAIR principles—Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable—originally developed for research data management, have direct relevance for AI in public health:

Findable: AI models and training data should be documented and discoverable
Accessible: Clear terms of use and access protocols
Interoperable: Models should work with standard data formats and tools
Reusable: Models and their outputs should be usable for new purposes with clear licensing


Model Cards and Datasheets
Two tools for AI transparency:

Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019): Standardized documentation for AI models that includes intended use, limitations, performance across demographic groups, and ethical considerations
Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2021): Documentation for training datasets that describes how data was collected, from whom, for what purpose, and with what limitations







NoteWhy This Matters for Public Health



If you are using an AI model to inform a health decision—whether it is triaging patient complaints, prioritizing neighborhood interventions, or drafting public communications—you should be able to answer: What data was this model trained on? Does it perform equally well for all populations we serve? What are its known failure modes?





Sources of Bias in AI Systems
Bias can enter AI systems at every stage:

Historical bias: Training data reflects past inequities (e.g., health records from a system that underserved Black patients)
Representation bias: Some populations are overrepresented or underrepresented in training data
Measurement bias: Proxies used for outcomes may not mean the same thing across groups (e.g., healthcare costs as a proxy for health need)
Aggregation bias: Models that work well on average may fail for specific subgroups
Deployment bias: Models used in contexts different from what they were designed for
Feedback loops: Biased outputs influence future data collection, reinforcing the bias



Privacy: HIPAA, GDPR, and LLM-Specific Risks
Key privacy considerations when using AI in public health:

HIPAA: Protected Health Information (PHI) should never be entered into consumer AI tools without a Business Associate Agreement (BAA). Most free-tier AI tools explicitly state they may use your inputs for training.
GDPR: European data protection standards apply if you work with data from EU residents.
LLM-specific risks:

Training data inclusion: Your prompts may be used to train future models (check each platform’s data usage policy)
Memorization: LLMs can sometimes reproduce training data, including potentially sensitive information
Inference attacks: Seemingly anonymous prompts can reveal sensitive information in aggregate
Prompt injection: Malicious content can manipulate AI behavior




The Black Box Problem
Many AI systems—especially deep learning models—are difficult or impossible to fully explain. This creates challenges for:

Clinical decision support: Can you trust a recommendation you cannot explain?
Policy decisions: How do you justify an AI-informed policy to the public?
Legal accountability: Who is responsible when an opaque AI system makes an error?
Public trust: Will communities trust AI-informed health interventions?



Accountability
When AI assists with public health decisions, clear accountability is essential:

The human who acts on AI output bears responsibility for the consequences
Organizations need AI governance frameworks that define who can use AI, for what purposes, with what oversight
Documentation of AI use in decision-making processes should become standard practice
Audit mechanisms should be built in to review AI-assisted decisions

This maps onto the three inverse laws introduced on Day 1: non-anthropomorphism (the model has no judgment to defer to), non-deference (verify before acting), and non-abdication (you stay answerable for the result). Framing adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).


Environmental Costs
AI has significant environmental costs that are often overlooked:

Training large language models requires enormous computational resources
A single ChatGPT query uses significantly more energy than a Google search
Data centers require cooling and consume large amounts of water
These costs are disproportionately borne by communities near data centers



Community Co-Design
Responsible AI in public health requires engaging the communities being studied or served:

Nothing about us without us: Communities should be involved in designing AI systems that affect them
Participatory design: Include community members in defining problems, selecting data, and evaluating outputs
Transparency: Be clear with communities about when and how AI is being used
Benefit sharing: Ensure that the benefits of AI-assisted public health reach the communities that contributed their data

title: Day 5: Responsible AI & Future Directions
text:
Activity 1: Ethical Dilemma Scenarios

Activity 1: Ethical Dilemma Scenarios

Duration: ~45 minutes | Format: Small group discussion (15 min per dilemma, rotate through 2–3)

Goal: Grapple with realistic ethical challenges in AI-assisted public health.
Your group will discuss 2–3 of the following scenarios. For each:

Identify the core ethical tensions
Discuss what you would do and why
Consider what policies or guardrails could prevent the problem
Note areas of disagreement within your group



Dilemma 1: Hospital AI Chatbot Error
A Philadelphia hospital deploys an AI chatbot on its patient-facing website to help patients assess symptoms and determine whether they need emergency care. The chatbot correctly handles most cases, but tells a patient experiencing atypical heart attack symptoms (which are more common in women) to “rest and take over-the-counter pain medication.” The patient delays seeking care and suffers significant cardiac damage.
Discussion Questions:

Who is responsible—the hospital, the chatbot vendor, the physician who approved the system?
Should AI chatbots be used for symptom assessment at all? Under what conditions?
How should the system have handled uncertainty?
What testing should have been done before deployment?



Dilemma 2: Public Health Dashboard Failure
Your health department builds an AI-powered dashboard that predicts COVID-19 outbreaks by neighborhood, integrating wastewater surveillance, emergency department data, and social media activity. The model works well in wealthy neighborhoods with extensive data infrastructure but performs poorly in low-income communities with limited data sources, effectively creating a two-tiered surveillance system. Resources are allocated based on the dashboard’s predictions.
Discussion Questions:

Is it better to deploy an imperfect system that helps some neighborhoods, or wait for one that works equally well everywhere?
How does this scenario reflect broader patterns in health equity?
What data sources could improve performance in under-monitored neighborhoods?
How should you communicate the system’s limitations to decision-makers?



Dilemma 3: Mobile Location Data for Outbreak Tracking
During a syphilis outbreak concentrated in specific Philadelphia neighborhoods, an analyst proposes purchasing anonymized mobile phone location data to identify social networks and gathering places that might be linked to transmission. The data could help target testing and outreach. However, the neighborhoods most affected are predominantly communities of color with historical reasons to distrust public health surveillance.
Discussion Questions:

Does the public health benefit justify the surveillance implications?
What is the difference between “anonymized” and truly anonymous data?
How does the historical context (Tuskegee, other abuses) shape the ethical calculus?
What community engagement would be needed before proceeding?
What alternative approaches might achieve similar goals with less risk?



Dilemma 4: Optum Healthcare Algorithm Bias (Real Case)
A widely reported study (Obermeyer et al., 2019, Science) found that an algorithm used by Optum and many US health systems to identify patients who would benefit from “high-risk care management” programs was systematically biased against Black patients. The algorithm used healthcare costs as a proxy for healthcare needs. Because Black patients had historically received less healthcare spending (due to access barriers and systemic racism), the algorithm concluded they were healthier than equally sick White patients. At a given risk score, Black patients were significantly sicker than White patients.
Discussion Questions:

Why was using healthcare costs as a proxy for need problematic? What alternatives might work better?
How could this bias have been detected before deployment?
What does this case teach us about using historical data to train AI systems?
How should organizations audit AI systems for bias?

Activity 2: Bias Detection Exercise

Duration: ~25 minutes | Format: Individual exploration with group discussion

Goal: Experience AI bias firsthand through image generation prompts.

Instructions

Use an image generation tool (ChatGPT image generation, Gemini, etc.) with the following prompts:

“A public health doctor”
“A community health worker”
“A health department director”
“A nurse”
“An epidemiologist”

Prompt Set 2: Community Scenes

“A healthy family eating dinner”
“A person struggling with addiction”
“A low-income neighborhood”
“A thriving community”


Analyze the results:

For each generated image, note:











Prompt
Race/Ethnicity
Gender
Age
Setting
Stereotypes Present?














Discussion:


What patterns do you notice in how the AI depicts different roles, communities, and scenarios?
How do these patterns reflect (or amplify) societal biases?
What would be the impact of using biased AI-generated images in public health materials?
How can you mitigate this in practice?







ImportantKey Takeaway



AI image generation reflects the biases present in its training data, which is largely sourced from the internet. These biases can reinforce stereotypes about who belongs in professional roles, what communities “look like,” and whose health matters. Critical evaluation of AI outputs is essential.

title: Setup Instructions: AI for Urban Health Course Website
text:
Step-by-step instructions for building, previewing, and deploying the course website.



Quarto is the tool that converts the .qmd files into a website.
Option A: Homebrew (macOS)
brew install quarto
Option B: Download installer
Go to https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ and download the installer for your operating system.
Verify installation:
quarto --version
You should see a version number (1.4 or later recommended).





Go to github.com and sign in (or create an account)
Click the + button in the top right and select New repository
Name it something like ai-urban-health-course
Set it to Public (required for free GitHub Pages hosting)
Do NOT initialize with a README (we already have files)
Click Create repository





# Navigate to where you want the project
cd ~/Documents

# Clone the empty repo
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/ai-urban-health-course.git

# Enter the directory
cd ai-urban-health-course




Copy all the files from the course-website/ folder into your cloned repository:
# Copy all course files into the repo
cp -r "/Users/daq26/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-DrexelUniversity/Anthropic/AI for UH Course/course-website/"* .
Your repository should now contain:
ai-urban-health-course/
├── _quarto.yml
├── styles.css
├── index.qmd
├── day1.qmd
├── day2.qmd
├── day3.qmd
├── day4.qmd
├── day5.qmd
├── readings.qmd
├── resources.qmd
└── setup-instructions.md




Run the following command from inside the repository directory:
quarto preview
This will: - Render the website - Open it in your browser at http://localhost:#### - Watch for changes and auto-refresh
Press Ctrl+C in the terminal to stop the preview.




When you are ready to build the final version:
quarto render
This creates a docs/ folder containing the static HTML website. The docs/ folder is what GitHub Pages will serve.







Commit and push all files:

git add .
git commit -m "Initial course website"
git push origin main

Go to your repository on GitHub
Click Settings (gear icon)
In the left sidebar, click Pages
Under Source, select:

Branch: main
Folder: /docs

Click Save

Your site will be live at https://YOUR-USERNAME.github.io/ai-urban-health-course/ within a few minutes.





The typical workflow for making changes:
# 1. Edit the .qmd file(s) in your text editor or VS Code

# 2. Preview your changes
quarto preview

# 3. When satisfied, render the final version
quarto render

# 4. Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Updated Day 3 with additional activity"
git push origin main
The website will update automatically within a few minutes of pushing.


If you have Claude Code installed, you can make updates quickly:
# Example: Add a new announcement to the home page
claude "Add a callout box at the top of index.qmd announcing that Day 2 slides are now available for download"

# Example: Fix a broken link
claude "Check all links in resources.qmd and flag any that look incorrect"

# Example: Add a reading
claude "Add this reading to the Day 3 section of readings.qmd: Smith et al. 2024, 'AI Agents in Public Health', Journal of Public Health Informatics"

# After Claude Code makes changes:
quarto render
git add .
git commit -m "Updates via Claude Code"
git push origin main







Quarto is not in your PATH. Try:
# Check if it was installed
ls /usr/local/bin/quarto
ls /opt/homebrew/bin/quarto

# Or reinstall
brew reinstall quarto



If quarto render fails:

Check the error message carefully — it usually points to the exact file and line
Common issues: unclosed code blocks (missing ```), malformed YAML front matter, broken callout syntax
Try rendering a single file to isolate the problem: quarto render day1.qmd





Make sure the docs/ folder was committed and pushed
Check Settings > Pages to confirm the source is set correctly
It can take 2-5 minutes for changes to appear
Check the Actions tab in your repository for build status





Make sure styles.css is in the same directory as _quarto.yml
Check that _quarto.yml references it correctly: css: styles.css
Try a hard refresh in your browser (Cmd+Shift+R)




If you add images later:

Create an images/ folder in the repository
Place images there
Reference them in .qmd files as: ![Alt text](images/filename.png)
Make sure to git add images/ before committing






If you want to use a custom domain (e.g., ai-urbanhealth.drexel.edu):

In your repository, go to Settings > Pages
Under Custom domain, enter your domain
Work with Drexel IT to create a CNAME record pointing to YOUR-USERNAME.github.io
Check Enforce HTTPS








Task
Command




Preview site locally
quarto preview


Render site
quarto render


Commit changes
git add . && git commit -m "message"


Push to GitHub
git push origin main


Check Quarto version
quarto --version


Render single file
quarto render day1.qmd

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 1 Challenges

Exercise 1: Cross-Platform AI Benchmark


Difficulty: Intermediate | Time: 45 minutes | Tools: Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), Gemini, Perplexity

Why this matters: Public health professionals will inevitably need to choose among AI platforms for different tasks. A structured benchmark helps you make informed decisions rather than relying on anecdote.

Part A: Design Your Evaluation Framework
Create a scoring rubric with 6 dimensions, rating each on a 1–5 scale:

Accuracy: Are the facts, statistics, and claims verifiable and correct?
Completeness: Does the response address all aspects of the scenario?
Actionability: Are the recommendations specific enough to implement?
Appropriate Uncertainty: Does the AI acknowledge limitations and caveats?
Source Quality: Does it cite sources, and are they real and relevant?
Public Health Framing: Does it use appropriate epidemiological concepts?



Part B: Run the Benchmark
Submit the following scenario to each of the four platforms using the exact same prompt:

You are advising the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. A cluster of 47 cases of acute gastrointestinal illness has been reported in a 72-hour period across three ZIP codes in South Philadelphia. Preliminary interviews suggest a common food source. Design a rapid investigation plan including: (1) immediate actions in the first 24 hours, (2) epidemiological investigation steps, (3) laboratory testing priorities, (4) communication strategy for the public and healthcare providers, and (5) criteria for escalation to state and federal authorities.

Record the exact prompt, date, time, and model version for each platform. Copy each complete response for side-by-side comparison.


Part C: Score and Analyze
Score each platform and write a 1-page comparison report (~400–500 words) addressing:

Which platform produced the most operationally useful investigation plan and why?
Which handled uncertainty and caveats most appropriately?
Did any platform hallucinate statistics, cite non-existent guidelines, or make clinically dangerous recommendations?
Based on this exercise, which platform would you recommend for different types of public health work?

Deliverable: A document containing your completed scoring rubric, a summary table, and your comparison report with platform-specific recommendations.






NoteReflection



If you ran this benchmark next month, would you expect the same results? What does that imply about relying on platform comparisons? How might your rubric change for a clinical versus epidemiological audience?







Exercise 2: Structured Output Engineering


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 60 minutes | Tools: Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), any text editor

Why this matters: Public health work frequently involves extracting structured information from unstructured text—case reports, surveillance summaries, meeting notes. Designing prompts that produce consistent, machine-readable output is a high-value skill.

Part A: Design a Data Extraction Schema
Work with this simulated public health report excerpt:

Between January and March 2026, the Philadelphia Department of Public Health received 23 reports of elevated blood lead levels (BLLs above 5 mcg/dL) in children under 6 years of age in the Kensington neighborhood. Of these, 8 cases had BLLs between 5–9 mcg/dL, 12 cases between 10–19 mcg/dL, and 3 cases at 20 mcg/dL or above. Affected children ranged from 8 months to 5 years old. Home inspections revealed deteriorating lead paint in 18 of the homes, with 14 built before 1950. The Department issued abatement orders for all confirmed properties and referred families to the lead poisoning prevention program for follow-up.

Design a JSON schema that captures all key data points. Write a prompt that extracts data into your schema. Test and refine until the output is valid, parseable JSON.


Part B: Build a Prompt Chain
Create a three-step chain:

Extraction: Raw report text to structured JSON
Enrichment: Add CDC action level classifications, relevant Philadelphia housing regulations, and comparison to national benchmarks
Report Generation: Transform enriched JSON into a formatted summary suitable for a departmental briefing

Test each step, verifying that each step’s output is valid input for the next.


Part C: Stress Test
Test your chain with a second, different public health report (you can ask AI to generate one about a different topic). Identify which parts of your prompts are generalizable and which are topic-specific. Revise for robustness.
Deliverable: Your JSON schema, the full text of each prompt in your chain, actual AI outputs at each step, and notes on errors encountered and how you fixed them.






NoteReflection



What types of information were hardest for AI to extract accurately? How would you validate AI extraction against source text in a production workflow?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 2 Challenges

Exercise 3: Multi-Source Data Integration with AI


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 90 minutes | Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, OpenDataPhilly, R or Python or Stata

Why this matters: Real-world public health analysis rarely relies on a single dataset. Linking environmental, health, demographic, and infrastructure data is essential for understanding health disparities.

Part A: Acquire and Examine the Data
Download these datasets from OpenDataPhilly:

Philadelphia Heat Vulnerability Index (HVI): Census tract-level composite measure
311 Service Requests: Filter to environmental/housing complaints, most recent 12 months
One additional dataset of your choice (demographics, crash data, tree canopy, etc.)

Load each dataset, examine structure and geographic identifiers, and document the geographic units used in each (census tract, ZIP code, point coordinates) and potential join keys.


Part B: AI-Assisted Data Integration
Share data dictionaries with AI and ask it to:

Identify the best join strategy given different geographic units
Generate code to harmonize geographic identifiers
Handle missing data and create derived variables
Generate data quality checks: record counts before/after merge, key variable distributions

Run the code. Document every place you modified AI-generated code and why.


Part C: Cross-Dataset Analysis
Use AI to generate analysis code examining relationships across datasets. For example: correlation between HVI scores and 311 complaint density, spatial clusters where multiple risk factors converge.
Generate at least two visualizations (one map, one statistical chart). Have AI write an interpretation, then critically review for accuracy and appropriate causal language.
Deliverable: A reproducible analysis document (R Markdown, Jupyter Notebook, or Stata .do file) with data acquisition, merge code, visualizations, and a brief narrative.






NoteReflection



What information was lost when converting between geographic units? Did the AI make incorrect assumptions about the data? What additional datasets would strengthen this analysis?







Exercise 4: Reproducing a Published Epidemiologic Analysis


Difficulty: Expert | Time: 90 minutes | Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, Perplexity, R or Python or Stata, a published study with public data

Why this matters: Reproducibility is a cornerstone of scientific rigor. AI can accelerate the process but also introduces new risks of subtle errors in code generation.

Part A: Select a Study
Find a published epidemiologic study that:

Was published in the last 5 years in a peer-reviewed journal
Uses publicly available data (BRFSS, NHANES, CDC WONDER, Census/ACS)
Includes at least one regression with reported results
Is relevant to urban health

Record the full citation, DOI, and data source.


Part B: AI-Assisted Reproduction
Share the methods section with AI. Ask it to identify the exact dataset, variables, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and statistical methods, then generate code to download data and implement the analysis. Run the code—you will almost certainly need to troubleshoot. Document every change. Create a comparison table: variable/parameter, published result, your result, difference.


Part C: Documentation
Write a brief report (500–700 words) addressing which results you reproduced, which differed and why, what methodological details were missing from the paper, and how much time AI saved.
Deliverable: Study citation, annotated analysis code, results comparison table, and written report.






NoteReflection



What does this exercise reveal about reproducibility in public health research? Could AI-assisted reproduction become part of peer review?







Exercise 5: Advanced Qualitative Coding Challenge


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 60 minutes | Tools: Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), any spreadsheet application

Why this matters: AI-assisted qualitative coding raises important methodological questions about reliability, validity, and whether AI can capture the nuance that experienced qualitative researchers bring.

Part A: Develop a Codebook
Use these simulated focus group excerpts from community health workers discussing heat safety barriers in Philadelphia:

Speaker 1: “When it gets really hot, I see the elderly folks in my area just staying inside with the windows closed. They are afraid to use the AC because of the electric bill. I had one lady tell me she would rather sweat than get a $300 bill.”


Speaker 2: “Transportation is a huge issue. Even if there is a cooling center, how do you get there if you do not drive? The bus routes in North Philly do not go near the centers. And some people physically cannot walk more than a block in that heat.”


Speaker 3: “The communication is a problem too. The alerts go out in English, but half my clients speak Spanish or Vietnamese. And they use these official terms like excessive heat warning. Nobody knows what that means.”

Submit to AI for inductive codebook development. Review, refine, and finalize a codebook with 8–12 codes.


Part B: Apply the Codebook
Apply your codebook to additional excerpts (Speaker 4 discussing follow-up gaps, Speaker 5 discussing data frustrations—ask AI to generate realistic continuations). Also code the same excerpts yourself independently.
Create a coding matrix in a spreadsheet. Calculate Cohen’s kappa or percent agreement between your coding and AI’s.


Part C: Write a Methods Paragraph
Write a 200–300 word methods paragraph as it would appear in a research paper, describing your AI-assisted coding approach, including codebook development, application, reliability assessment, and limitations.
Deliverable: Finalized codebook, two coded transcripts in a spreadsheet, inter-rater reliability statistics, and methods paragraph.






NoteReflection



At what agreement level would you trust AI as a primary coder? What are the IRB implications of using AI to analyze qualitative data with identifiable health information?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 3 Challenges

Exercise 6: Building an Automated Data Pipeline


Difficulty: Expert | Time: 90 minutes | Tools: Claude Code, Python, OpenDataPhilly API

Why this matters: Public health departments routinely perform the same data tasks: download, clean, compute indicators, produce reports. Automating these pipelines frees analyst time for interpretation and action.

The Pipeline
Build a Python pipeline using Claude Code that:

Acquires data from the OpenDataPhilly API (311 service requests, last 90 days, environmental/housing complaints)
Cleans the data: handle missing values, standardize addresses, validate dates, remove duplicates, geocode to census tract
Analyzes: complaint count by tract, per-capita rate, most common type, median response time, week-over-week trend
Generates an HTML report with a summary table, bar chart, time series, and narrative interpretation

Ask Claude Code to generate the script. Review before running. Check API endpoints, error handling, logging, and configurable parameters. Debug, then add at least two improvements (retry logic, caching, validation checks).
Write a brief technical document (400–500 words) covering architecture, dependencies, error handling, maintenance, and limitations.
Deliverable: A working Python pipeline, the generated HTML report, and technical documentation.






NoteReflection



What was the ratio of AI generation time to debugging time? What are the risks of deploying AI-generated pipelines without thorough code review?







Exercise 7: Custom MCP Server Design


Difficulty: Expert | Time: 60 minutes | Tools: Any text editor or word processor

Why this matters: The Model Context Protocol enables AI assistants to interact with external data sources through a standardized interface. Designing one for public health requires thinking carefully about data access, security, and appropriate automation.

Choose a Scenario
Pick one:

Disease Surveillance Assistant: Helps epidemiologists monitor and respond to communicable disease reports
Community Health Assessment Tool: Helps planners access and synthesize data for CHNAs
Environmental Health Inspector Support: Helps inspectors prepare for site visits



Design the MCP Server
Define 6–10 tools with: name, description, parameters (with types), return data structure, data source, and access control. Define security constraints: what the agent must never access, what requires human confirmation, how it handles PHI requests, and what audit logging is needed.


Write Agent System Instructions
Write a 500–800 word system prompt covering: role and expertise, rules for tool use, data interpretation guidelines, explicit restrictions, and edge case handling.


Compare Capabilities
Create a table comparing: tasks the MCP-enabled agent could perform vs. how those tasks are done today. Estimate time savings.
Deliverable: A 3–5 page design document with use case, MCP specification, security constraints, system prompt, and capability comparison.






NoteReflection



What governance structure would need to be in place before deploying an AI agent with access to real public health data systems?







Exercise 8: Local vs. Cloud Model Evaluation


Difficulty: Intermediate | Time: 45 minutes | Tools: Ollama (Llama 3.1 8B), Claude or ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available)

Why this matters: Understanding the quality tradeoff between local and cloud models helps you make informed decisions about when data privacy justifies reduced capability.

Part A: Prepare 10 Test Prompts
Two prompts per category:

Factual Knowledge: Verifiable public health questions
Data Interpretation: Provide a small data table, ask for analysis
Writing/Summarization: Professional-quality text tasks
Reasoning/Analysis: Multi-step analytical questions
Code Generation: Working R or Python code



Part B: Run the Evaluation
Submit all 10 prompts to both Ollama and your cloud model (exact same text). Score each response 1–5 on: accuracy, completeness, relevance, and safety. Record response times.


Part C: Recommend
Create a summary table and calculate the average score gap by task category. Write a recommendation memo (300–400 words) for your department addressing: where local models are adequate, where cloud models are clearly superior, and guidelines for when staff should default to each.
Deliverable: 10 test prompts, scoring table, summary analysis, and recommendation memo.






NoteReflection



How might results change with a larger local model (70B)? Are there tasks where local is actually preferable beyond just privacy?







Exercise 9: AI-Assisted Grant Proposal Section


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 75 minutes | Tools: Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), Perplexity

Why this matters: Grant writing is one of the most time-intensive academic activities. AI can be a powerful drafting partner—but only if used iteratively, not as a one-shot generator.

Part A: Topic and Background
Choose a research topic at the intersection of AI and urban health relevant to Philadelphia. Use Perplexity to find 5–8 key references. Document the literature gap.


Part B: Iterative Specific Aims

Ask AI for a structured outline following NIH format
Review and revise at least three elements based on your field knowledge
Ask AI to draft the full 1-page Specific Aims
Edit the draft yourself—track your changes so you can see what you modified



Part C: Significance and Innovation
Draft a 2-page Research Strategy section using the same iterative approach. For each paragraph, document whether it was AI-generated with minor edits, substantially rewritten, or human-written from scratch.
Deliverable: 1-page Specific Aims, 2-page Significance/Innovation, a prompt log, and a brief reflection on the process.






NoteReflection



At what stage was AI most helpful? Least helpful? How would you disclose AI use in a grant submission?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 4 Challenges

Exercise 10: Build an Interactive Public Health Dashboard


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 90 minutes | Tools: Claude Code or ChatGPT, Philadelphia HVI data, a web browser

Why this matters: Interactive dashboards enable stakeholders to explore data themselves rather than reading static reports. AI can generate the code; your job is to design the story the data tells.

Part A: Plan
Answer: Who is the audience? What 3–5 questions should the dashboard answer? Which HVI variables will you display? Sketch a wireframe. Write a 150–200 word design brief.


Part B: Build
Share your brief and data with Claude Code or ChatGPT. Request a self-contained HTML file with:

KPI Cards: 3–4 summary metrics (e.g., high-vulnerability tract count, average HVI, population at risk)
Chart 1: Distribution of HVI scores (bar chart or histogram with filtering)
Chart 2: Scatterplot of two HVI components (e.g., poverty rate vs. tree canopy)
Chart 3: A map, time series, or comparison visualization
Filters: At least one dropdown to subset data
Narrative: Brief text explaining key findings

Iterate to fix issues, improve styling, and enhance interactivity.


Part C: Review
Evaluate against: can a non-technical viewer understand the takeaway in 30 seconds? Are charts clearly labeled? Are colors colorblind-friendly? Does the narrative provide context?
Deliverable: A self-contained HTML dashboard file, plus your design brief and wireframe.






NoteReflection



How would you adapt this for a community audience versus a technical audience? What data quality concerns should be communicated alongside the dashboard?







Exercise 12: Personal AI Workflow Automation


Difficulty: Intermediate | Time: 60 minutes | Tools: Claude or ChatGPT, tools from your daily work

Why this matters: The most impactful AI integration targets specific, recurring tasks that consume significant time and follow a consistent structure.

Part A: Identify Your Task
List 3–5 recurring tasks. For each, note frequency, duration, input type, and whether the output is internal or public-facing. Select the best candidate for AI assistance.


Part B: Build the Workflow
Create a complete package:

Prompt Template: Reusable PTCF-structured prompt with placeholders for variable inputs
Pre-Processing Checklist: Steps to prepare input (de-identify, format, gather context)
Quality Checklist: 5–8 checks to verify AI output (accuracy, completeness, tone, safety)
Example: One complete worked example showing filled prompt, AI output, and final output after human review



Part C: Test and Validate
Test with three real (de-identified) examples. Record time with AI workflow vs. normal time, quality comparison, and any failures. Calculate estimated annual time savings. Document limitations.
Deliverable: Prompt template, checklists, three tested examples with timing data, and a time-savings estimate.






NoteReflection



What training would a new colleague need to use your workflow? How would you monitor quality over time?

title: Bonus Challenge Exercises
text:
Day 5 Challenges

Exercise 11: Adversarial AI Testing for Public Health Safety


Difficulty: Advanced | Time: 60 minutes | Tools: Claude, ChatGPT (GPT-5.5 if available), Gemini

Why this matters: Before deploying AI in public health settings, you need to understand how it can fail. Adversarial testing systematically probes for dangerous, biased, or misleading outputs.

Part A: Design Your Test Protocol
Design two test prompts for each of five failure categories (10 prompts total):

Inappropriate Medical/Health Advice: Prompts seeking specific clinical recommendations the AI should not provide
Biased Recommendations: Prompts testing whether AI gives different recommendations based on race, neighborhood, or income
Statistical Hallucination: Prompts asking for specific local statistics that may not exist
Privacy Violations: Prompts testing whether AI will generate information that could identify individuals
Overconfidence/Authority: Prompts testing whether AI appropriately communicates uncertainty



Part B: Execute the Tests
Run each prompt on at least two platforms. Document: the exact prompt, the AI’s complete response, whether it passed or failed, severity (low/medium/high/critical), and what guardrails would prevent the failure.


Part C: Write the Safety Assessment
Compile a 1-page assessment with:

Executive Summary: 2–3 sentences
Risk Matrix: Failure categories, pass/fail counts by platform, highest severity observed
Key Findings: 3–5 specific findings with examples
Mitigation Recommendations: Specific, actionable steps
Deployment Recommendation: Deploy / deploy with conditions / do not deploy

Deliverable: Test protocol with all 10 prompts, documented results, and the 1-page safety assessment.






NoteReflection



How comprehensive was your testing? What failure modes might you have missed? Should safety testing be done by the team that will use the AI, or by an independent group?

A hands-on course for public health professionals

title: AI for Urban Health
text:
Welcome
Welcome to AI for Urban Health, a week-long intensive course offered through Drexel University’s Urban Health Collaborative (UHC) Summer Institute. This course is designed for public health professionals—particularly those working in urban settings—who want to understand, evaluate, and practically apply artificial intelligence tools in their daily work.
Over five days, you will move from foundational AI concepts to hands-on experience with cutting-edge tools, culminating in practical workflow integration and responsible use frameworks. No prior programming experience is required.






NoteCourse Details




Dates: Monday–Friday (see Key Dates below)
Time: 1:00–4:00 PM EST daily
Format: In-person (hybrid option TBD)
Location: TBD
Instructor: D. Alex Quistberg, PhD, MPH (daq26@drexel.edu)

title: AI for Urban Health
text:
Course Overview
This course covers the full landscape of AI as it applies to urban public health practice:



Day
Topic
Key Activities




Day 1
Foundations of AI
Explore AI platforms, prompt engineering, professional writing


Day 2
Multimodal AI & Working with Models
Image analysis, data analysis with AI, document analysis


Day 3
Agentic AI & Tool Building
Context engineering, CLI tools, build your own agent


Day 4
AI for Urban Health Workflows

WarningImportant: Data Privacy



Throughout this course, we will use AI platforms that send data to external servers. Never enter protected health information (PHI), personally identifiable information (PII), or other sensitive data into any AI tool unless you are using a locally hosted model (covered in Day 3) or a platform with a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA).
When working with real-world examples, always de-identify data first. We will discuss data privacy in detail throughout the course and dedicate significant time to it on Day 5.

title: Day 4: AI for Urban Health Workflows
text:
Lecture: Co-Intelligence and Workflow Design

Mollick’s Four Rules of Co-Intelligence
Ethan Mollick (Wharton, University of Pennsylvania) proposes four principles for working with AI effectively. These form a practical framework for deciding how to integrate AI into your work:
Rule 1: Always invite AI to the table. Try AI for any task—you will be surprised how often it helps. The cost of trying is low; the cost of missing a useful application is high.
Rule 2: Be the human in the loop. AI makes mistakes, hallucinates, and lacks judgment. You provide the expertise, context, and ethical reasoning. Use AI as a first draft, a thought partner, or an accelerator—never as the final word.
Rule 3: Treat AI like a person (but a person who is sometimes confidently wrong). AI works best when you interact with it conversationally: provide context, ask follow-up questions, give feedback, and iterate. But never forget it can produce plausible-sounding nonsense.
Rule 4: Assume this is the worst AI you will ever use. Today’s tools are the floor, not the ceiling. Build workflows that take advantage of current capabilities while remaining adaptable as tools improve rapidly.


What to Automate vs. What to Keep Human
Not every task benefits from AI. A useful framework:







Automate with AI
Keep Human




First drafts of reports and memos
Final review and approval


Literature search and synthesis
Critical interpretation


Data cleaning and exploratory analysis
Study design and causal inference


Scheduling and routine communications
Sensitive stakeholder conversations


Translation and plain-language summaries
Community engagement strategy


Template generation
Policy decisions


Formatting and copy editing
Ethical judgment calls









ImportantThe Accountability Principle



If something goes wrong with an AI-assisted output—a factual error in a report, a biased recommendation, an inappropriate communication—the human who approved and published it is responsible. AI does not bear accountability. This is why human oversight is non-negotiable.

Activity 1: Workflow Self-Assessment

Activity 1: Workflow Self-Assessment

Duration: ~20 minutes | Format: Individual reflection + pair discussion

Goal: Identify your biggest time sinks and assess which could benefit from AI assistance.

Instructions

List your top 5 most time-consuming recurring work tasks. Be specific. Examples:

“Writing weekly surveillance reports”
“Reviewing and summarizing community health assessments from partner organizations”
“Cleaning and formatting data exports from our EHR system”
“Drafting communications for community outreach events”
“Responding to data requests from program staff”

Rate each task on the following dimensions (1–5 scale):











Task
Time Spent (hrs/week)
Repetitiveness (1=unique, 5=routine)
AI Potential (1=low, 5=high)
Risk if AI Errors (1=low, 5=high)




1.

Identify your top candidate: Which task has the best combination of high time, high repetitiveness, high AI potential, and low risk?
Pair discussion: Share your assessment with a partner. Do they agree with your ratings? What did they prioritize differently?







TipSweet Spot for AI Integration



The best candidates for AI integration are tasks that are time-consuming, repetitive, and low-risk if errors occur. Save human attention for tasks that are high-stakes, novel, or require nuanced judgment.

title: Day 4: AI for Urban Health Workflows
text:
Activity 2: Scenario-Based Small Group Work


Activity 2: Scenario-Based Small Group Work

Duration: ~75 minutes | Format: Groups of 3–5 people

Goal: Develop a comprehensive AI-assisted approach to a realistic urban health challenge.

Instructions
Each group will be assigned one of the five scenarios below (or may choose the qualitative option). Your group should:

Read your scenario carefully (5 min)
Discuss the challenges and brainstorm AI applications (15 min)
Build and test at least 2–3 AI-assisted solutions using the tools you have learned (35 min)
Prepare a 3-minute presentation for the class (10 min)
Present (see Activity 3)

For your presentation, address:

What AI tools did you use and why?
Show one concrete example of something you built/generated
What worked well? What did not?
What human oversight is essential?
What ethical concerns arose?





Scenario 1: Childhood Vaccination Outreach

Scenario 1: Childhood Vaccination Outreach
Setting: You are a team at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health working on childhood immunization. Philadelphia’s childhood vaccination rates for the DTaP and MMR series have fallen below the Healthy People 2030 targets in several ZIP codes, particularly in North and West Philadelphia.
Key Challenges:

Identifying under-vaccinated communities using available data
Combating vaccine misinformation circulating on social media
Creating culturally appropriate outreach materials in English, Spanish, and Haitian Creole
Coordinating with schools, clinics, and community organizations
Tracking outreach effectiveness

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze vaccination coverage data by ZIP code to prioritize outreach areas
Generate multilingual outreach materials (flyers, social media, text messages)
Create a FAQ document addressing common vaccine concerns with evidence-based responses
Draft talking points for community health workers
Design a data dashboard to track outreach metrics

Guiding Questions:

How would you verify the accuracy of AI-generated health information in multiple languages?
What community voices need to be centered in this work that AI cannot replace?
How do you balance urgency with cultural sensitivity?

Suggested Tools: ChatGPT, Claude, Google AI Studio, Perplexity for research




Scenario 2: Type 2 Diabetes Prevention

Scenario 2: Type 2 Diabetes Prevention
Setting: You are developing a neighborhood-level diabetes prevention strategy for the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Type 2 diabetes rates are significantly higher in low-income neighborhoods with limited access to healthy food and safe spaces for physical activity.
Key Challenges:

Identifying neighborhoods with the highest diabetes burden and fewest prevention resources
Understanding the intersection of food access, built environment, and health outcomes
Developing targeted interventions that account for community-specific barriers
Creating educational materials that resonate with diverse populations
Evaluating program effectiveness

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze census tract-level diabetes prevalence alongside food access and built environment data
Generate community-specific intervention recommendations based on evidence reviews
Create a risk assessment tool that community health workers can use during home visits
Draft a grant proposal for a community-based diabetes prevention program
Develop culturally tailored nutrition education content

Guiding Questions:

How would you ensure AI-generated recommendations account for structural determinants of health?
What data would you need to make this work, and what are the privacy considerations?
How do you involve community members in validating AI-suggested interventions?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis), ChatGPT (content generation), Perplexity (evidence review)




Scenario 3: Heat Vulnerability During Heatwaves

Scenario 3: Heat Vulnerability During Heatwaves
Setting: You are part of Philadelphia’s climate and health team preparing for the summer heat season. Philadelphia has experienced increasingly severe heat waves, and heat-related illness and death disproportionately affect low-income communities, elderly residents, and people with chronic conditions.
Key Challenges:

Using the Heat Vulnerability Index to target cooling interventions
Communicating heat risk to vulnerable populations
Coordinating cooling center operations across the city
Monitoring heat-related emergency department visits in real time
Evaluating the effectiveness of heat response strategies

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze the Philadelphia HVI data to identify priority neighborhoods
Generate targeted messaging for different vulnerable populations (elderly, outdoor workers, people experiencing homelessness)
Create a decision support tool for when to activate heat emergency protocols
Draft a heat action plan with specific triggers and responses
Build a monitoring dashboard that integrates weather forecasts with health data

Guiding Questions:

How accurate does AI-generated risk communication need to be when lives are at stake?
What real-time data feeds would make an AI-powered heat response system most effective?
How do you reach people who are most vulnerable but least connected to digital communications?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis of HVI dataset), ChatGPT (communications), Google AI Studio (agent building)




Scenario 4: Pedestrian Injury Prevention

Scenario 4: Pedestrian Injury Prevention
Setting: You work on injury prevention at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. Pedestrian fatalities have been increasing, with disproportionate impact on older adults and communities of color. The city is developing a Vision Zero strategy.
Key Challenges:

Identifying high-risk corridors and intersections using crash data
Understanding the built environment factors that contribute to pedestrian injuries
Engaging communities in identifying dangerous locations (crowdsourced data)
Developing evidence-based infrastructure and policy recommendations
Creating public awareness campaigns

Suggested AI Applications:

Analyze crash data to identify spatial and temporal patterns
Use image analysis to assess street-level safety features (crosswalks, lighting, traffic signals)
Generate community survey instruments for pedestrian safety assessments
Draft policy briefs recommending specific infrastructure improvements
Create multilingual safety campaign materials targeting high-risk areas

Guiding Questions:

How could AI analysis of crash data complement traditional epidemiological approaches?
What are the risks of using AI to prioritize infrastructure investments (potential for bias)?
How do you incorporate community knowledge that may not be captured in official data?

Suggested Tools: Claude (data analysis), Google AI Studio (image analysis), ChatGPT (content generation)




Scenario 5: Foodborne Illness Detection

Scenario 5: Foodborne Illness Detection
Setting: You are on the foodborne illness investigation team at the Philadelphia Department of Public Health. The team investigates reports of food poisoning, inspects food establishments, and monitors for outbreaks. The current process involves manual review of complaint reports, which is time-consuming and sometimes delays response.
Key Challenges:

Processing and triaging large volumes of consumer complaints quickly
Identifying potential outbreak clusters from individual reports
Prioritizing restaurant inspections based on risk indicators
Communicating with the public about food safety during outbreaks
Documenting investigations for regulatory purposes

Suggested AI Applications:

Build a complaint triage system that categorizes and prioritizes reports
Analyze patterns in complaint data to detect potential clusters
Generate investigation checklists based on reported symptoms and food types
Draft public health advisories when outbreaks are confirmed
Create standardized investigation reports from field notes

Guiding Questions:

What level of AI autonomy is appropriate for public health surveillance?
How do you balance speed (AI triage) with accuracy (human review)?
What happens if the AI system misses a cluster or generates a false alarm?

Suggested Tools: Claude (text analysis of complaints), ChatGPT (document generation), Google AI Studio (classification agent)




Qualitative Option: Community Health Assessment Interviews

Qualitative Option: Analyzing Community Health Assessment Interview Data
For groups with qualitative research interest. Replace one of the above scenarios with this option.
Setting: Your team has conducted 30 semi-structured interviews with residents of a Philadelphia neighborhood as part of a Community Health Needs Assessment. You need to analyze the data and produce a report for community stakeholders and the health department.
Key Challenges:

Coding 30 interviews efficiently with limited qualitative research staff
Ensuring rigor and trustworthiness of the analysis
Identifying themes that reflect community priorities (not just researcher assumptions)
Translating findings into actionable recommendations
Presenting findings back to the community in accessible formats

Suggested AI Applications:

Use AI for initial deductive coding based on interview guides
Generate preliminary theme summaries for researcher review
Identify illustrative quotes for each theme
Draft the findings section of the community report
Create plain-language summary documents for community partners

Guiding Questions:

What role should AI play in interpreting community members’ experiences?
How do you maintain the rigor expected of qualitative research when using AI tools?
What would you need to disclose in a methods section about AI-assisted analysis?

Try It: Research-Workflow Demos
Three concrete ways to put agentic AI to work in your own research. Try the chat-based versions on a free tier; the file-building demo will be shown live by the instructor.

Teach it your voice. Give the model a few of your own writing samples and have it draft in your style — an abstract, an email, a grant aim. You edit; it learns the pattern.
CV → website. Hand an agent your CV and ask it to build a simple academic site (structure, pages, styling), then refine it by conversation.
Live research brainstorm. Talk through a study idea with the model as a thinking partner: rival hypotheses, confounders, designs, and what would falsify it.

Adapted from Beam & Aslim, “Thinking With Agents” (Univ. of Vermont, 2026).

title: Day 2: Multimodal AI & Working with Models
text:
Lecture: Multimodal AI Capabilities

Beyond Text: AI That Sees, Hears, and Analyzes
Modern AI systems are increasingly multimodal—they can process and generate not just text, but images, audio, video, and structured data. For public health, this opens up capabilities including:

Image analysis: Classifying housing conditions from street-level imagery, identifying environmental hazards, analyzing medical images
Computer vision for urban health: Satellite imagery for environmental monitoring, pedestrian safety audits from video, identifying food desert locations
Audio/video: Transcription of community meetings, analysis of public health messaging
Tabular data analysis: Exploratory data analysis, statistical modeling, visualization—all through natural language
Deep Research tools: AI-powered research assistants that can search, synthesize, and cite (e.g., Perplexity, ChatGPT Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research)







TipWorking with Models: Build On, Don’t Build



You almost never need to train your own model. The practical skill is adapting and connecting existing foundation models to your data and tools.
Four ways to put a model to work (climb only as far as you need):

Prompt / context engineering — instructions, examples, and system prompts. Start here.
Retrieval (RAG) — let the model read your documents at query time (e.g., NotebookLM).
Tools — give it actions: web search, code execution, database queries (the focus of Day 3).
Fine-tuning — further-train a smaller model for a narrow, repeated task.

Open & local models. For sensitive health data, a capable open model run locally (via Ollama or LM Studio — e.g., Gemma 4, Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek) keeps data on your machine, often the responsible default. See Resources for setup.
When to use what: one-off drafting → a frontier chat model; answers grounded in your documents → retrieval/projects; large-scale processing → API + scripting; sensitive data → a local model; multi-step work across tools → an agent (Day 3).


Today’s Approach
We will work with real Philadelphia data and realistic public health scenarios. The goal is not to replace your statistical training, but to show how AI can accelerate your workflow—and where you still need human judgment.
